coredata.dc:title: Nanobody mediated dual-mode immunoassay for detection of peanut allergen Ara h 3
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Chixuan
Hu, Yaozhong
Liu, Qisijing
Liu, Jing-Min
Ji, Xuemeng
Lv, Huan
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: To improve the performance of peanut allergen Ara h 3 detection, depending on boron and nitrogen carbon dots (B/N-CDs), a nanobody (Nb) mediated dual-mode immunoassay was established, which combines the dominance of colorimetry with ratiometric fluorescence techniques. With the catalysis of Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the oxidization of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) in the presence of H2O2, leading to the production of yellow 2,3-diaminophenolazine (DAP) with an absorption peak at 431 nm. Owing to inner filter effect (IFE), DAP quenched the fluorescence of B/N-CDs at 426 nm, and it emerged a new emission peak at 549 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio and absorption intensity can be utilized for quantitative analysis of Ara h 3 concentration. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits were 6.61 and 9.79 ng·mL−1, respectively. The dual-mode immunoassay was assessed containing specificity, stability, reproducibility, and practicability. This method paved the way for sensitive detection of Ara h 3 without background Interference.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetry
Ratiometric fluorescence
Immunoassay
Nanobody
Peanut allergen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedures for dual-mode immunoassay of Ara h 3
The method applied for real food specimens
The colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensing mechanism for Ara h 3
The optimal conditions for the detection
Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Ara h 3
Selectivity of the dual-mode immunoassay for Ara h 3 determinaton

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China
Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China, grant number 2022YFF1102400.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Ara h 3.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of B/N-CDs and APBA. (B) Fluorescence spectra of B/N-CDs excited from 320 to 420 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of B/N-CDs and UV−vis absorption spectra of DAP and o-PD. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP solution in the absence and presence of B/N-CDs. (C) The fluorescence lifetime spectra of B/N-CDs in the absence and presence of DAP.

(A) and (B) were the ratio of fluorescence intensities (f549/f426 ) and their linear response ranges for Ara h 3. (C) and (D) were the plots and the linear range of absorbance measured at 431 nm as a function of the Ara h 3 concentration, separately. The error bars were calculated based on the SD of three batches.

The proposed ELISA was performed to detect binding specificity of selected Nb and the potential cross-reactivity with irrelevant proteins of Ara h 1, β-LG, κ-CN, and OVA. All data are expressed as mean ± SD (n = 3). Each test was repeated three times at least.
Comparison of various immunoassays employed for food allergen sensing.

head.ce:title: Nanobody mediated dual-mode immunoassay for detection of peanut allergen Ara h 3

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the performance of peanut allergen Ara h 3 detection, depending on boron and nitrogen carbon dots (B/N-CDs), a nanobody (Nb) mediated dual-mode immunoassay was established, which combines the dominance of colorimetry with ratiometric fluorescence techniques. With the catalysis of Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the oxidization of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) in the presence of H2O2, leading to the production of yellow 2,3-diaminophenolazine (DAP) with an absorption peak at 431 nm. Owing to inner filter effect (IFE), DAP quenched the fluorescence of B/N-CDs at 426 nm, and it emerged a new emission peak at 549 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio and absorption intensity can be utilized for quantitative analysis of Ara h 3 concentration. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits were 6.61 and 9.79 ng·mL−1, respectively. The dual-mode immunoassay was assessed containing specificity, stability, reproducibility, and practicability. This method paved the way for sensitive detection of Ara h 3 without background Interference.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The prevalence of food allergies was continuously increasing over the years. Death, served as a ending of anaphylactic reactions, is more likely as an outcome of peanut allergies than any of other food allergies (Dodo, Marsic, Callender, Cebert, & Viquez, 2002). Keeping the high-risk population off all the products containing peanut ingredients is extremely a huge challenge, given that peanuts are a kind of prevalent food ingredients, making unforeseen ingestion possible. However about 20 kinds of peanut allergens have been reported, Ara h 3 are classified as one of the important major allergic agents, which influenced about 50 % of peanut allergic patients (Rabjohn et al., 1999). Further, during the food production, trace concentration of contamination between raw materials process can cause peanut allergy symptoms, so that the sensitivity of conventional ELISA according to the previous work is difficult to meet the demands. It is an urgent need to establish a more sensitive method for detection in possibly contaminated food stuff.

Immunoassays are the frequently-used analytical techniques to evaluate the concentrations of food allergens in a range of industries (Wu et al., 2016). Among immunoassays, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) has been most used because of its overwhelming dominance of highly selective and sensitive, flexible operability, quick response, high throughput, low cost, safety, and universality. ELISA primarily depended on the highly specific antigen–antibody binding reaction. In ELISA, the highly effective biocatalytic reactions based on antigen–antibody binding reaction could deliver chromogenic, fluorescence, and chemiluminescence readout signals to reflect the analytes quantificationally. In the past, ELISA has been successfully used to detect food allergens in several food products such as in meat, milk, fish, soybean, nuts and their-based products (Do, Khuda, & Sharma, 2018). Nevertheless, both industrial and academic circles have turned some of this attention towards the reproducibility and repeatability issue of antibodies due to the limited supply and large diversity between batches of polyclonal antibodies or the degeneration and loss of hybridomas cells of monoclonal antibodies during storage (Baker, 2015). Therefore, there is an urgent need that “all antibody reagents should be sequenced and then produced from the genetic code obtained” (Bao, Tang, Zhao, & Zhu, 2021). Nanobody (Nb) are single-domain heavy chain-only antibodies derived from camelids (Yélamos, 2022). Nb was capable of distinguishing the targets as same as the conventional antibodies with good affinity and specificity. Since then, numerous Nb has been developed and applied in the fields of medical diagnosis, therapy, and environmental monitoring (Ji et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Schoof et al., 2020). Compared with the conventional antibodies, Nb possesses the distinctive superiorities as follow: Nb has good antigenicity towards cryptic epitopes by reason of their smaller size (∼15 kDa) and single-domain nature; Nb still has high activity under harsh environment and resistance to thermal and chemical denaturation as before (Muyldermans, 2013). These superiorities make Nb a hopeful recognized element in the new generation of immunoassays to multifarious targets, including biotoxin, viruses, and biomarkers (Chen et al., 2016; He, Li, Gao, Tong, & Chen, 2018; Mao et al., 2022; Ren et al., 2019; Sheng et al., 2019).

Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) has become an extensively used labeling enzyme in ELISA to catalyze the oxidation of substrate conversion to the coloured production, and the signal response is recorded by UV–VIS spectroscopy. O-phenylenediamine (o-PD) can be selected as the HRP substrate so that colorless o-PD can be oxidized efficiently by HRP/H2O2 to form 2,3-diaminophenolazine (DAP), which has a strong yellow fluorescence emission (Zuo, Xia, Li, Sun, Zhao, & Zhu, 2022).

According to this feature, tremendously to satisfy the demand of highly sensitive detection toward peanut allergen, an efficacious strategy is to build a sensor platform provided with a dual-mode signal, such as colorimetry and fluorescence, in order to possess a visualization effect and high sensitivity. The colorimetry method was common and intuitionistic with background interference. Ratiometric fluorescent method could detect the targets through measuring the change of the ratios of fluorescence intensities by two different emission wavelengths. It could provide a correction to the background interference. The ratiometric fluorescent sensors were established with the participation of appropriate sensing materials, which could improve the deficiency the colorimetry method. (Chandrasekaran, Aswathy, James, Kala, Ragi, & Manoj, 2021; Feng, Bai, Jia, & Feng, 2018; Mergu, Moon, Kim, Heo, & Son, 2018). Compared with these well-behaved sensing materials, heteroatom doped carbon dots have also growing as fluorescent probes owing to their good chemical stability, excellent fluorescence performance (Sun & Lei, 2017). For the outstanding properties of heteroatom-doped carbon dots, it is advantageous to construct ratiometric fluorescence method for the detection of peanut allergen Ara h 3. Since the detection of Ara h 3 was still a huge challenge, the integration of heteroatom-doped carbon dots and Nb to construct the dual-mode ELISA can solve this problem.

Due to the allergic reaction to peanut allergen, the research of Ara h 3 measurement must be assured. So far, few studies have reported the strategies of measuring peanut allergen Ara h 3. Some methods were assessed for their ability to recover peanut from the standard (Jayasena et al., 2015; Pandey, Varshney, Sudini, & Pandey, 2019). It is of great significance to make a profound study the accuracy and selectivity of immunoassay. Herein, for the first time, a nanobody-mediated dual-mode optical sensing immunoassay for peanut allergen Ara h 3 are established. The present study aimed to effectively eliminate the background interference of complex food matrices. Using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the peroxidase substrate, as well as boron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (B/N-CDs) a sensing probe, in order to establish a highly sensitive method on account of the inner filter effect (IFE) principle for the accurate analysis of Ara h 3 in food samples (in Fig. 1 ). The designed method presented marked upgradation in detection limit contrasted with the general ELISA. The specificity, accuracy, and precision of this novel ELISA were evaluated by detecting peanut spiked food specimens. In addition, the novel ELISA results for Ara h 3 detection in different food samples.

The Ara h 3 standard was purchased from Indoor Biotechnologies, Inc. Anti-Ara h 3 nanobody P43 (named P43 in the following text) were from our in-house collection (Hu et al., 2023). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %, w/v) and Na2B4O7·10H2O was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), β-Lactoglobulin (β-LG), κ-casein(κ-CN) and chicken ovalbumin (OVA) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Mouse anti-HA IgG and HRP- conjugated goat anti-mouse IgGs were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, USA). The manufacturer of 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA), o-phenylenediamine (o-PD), Tween 20, sodium acetate and NaOH were Meryer Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Food samples (cookie, cake and chocolate) were purchased from the local market (Tianjin, China). The rest chemical agents used in this work were of analytical grade unless specified otherwise. The ultrapure-water was obtained through a Milli–Q system (Billerica, MA, USA).

B/ N-CDs were synthetized by means of a one-step hydrothermal process using APBA as the carbon, boron and nitrogen source (Xiao et al., 2018). 5.1 mg of APBA was fully dissolved in 5.1 mL ultrapure water. NaOH was used to regulate the pH of mixture to 10.0 under continuous stirring. The obtained mixture was heated at 160 °C for 8 h in the sealed-Teflon autoclave. Afterwards, the autoclave chilled to ambient temperature, the product was isolated by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) and filtered by filter paper. The B/N-CDs powder was achieved by vacuum freeze-drying, and further mixed with ultrapure water with the final content of 5 mg·mL−1 which was stored at 4℃ for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for dual-mode immunoassay of Ara h 3
ce:section.ce:para: For the development of the colormetric-ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay, Nb P43 with His-tag and both tags (His-and HA-tag) were used as the capture and detection antibodies, separately. 150 μL of P43 with His-tag (capture antibody, 8 μg·mL−1) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.4) was added to the wells of 96-well ELISA plates, and incubated at room temperature (RT) for 1 h. After washing five times with 250 μL washing buffer (PBS containing 0.05 % Tween-20, PBST), the wells were blocked with the blocking buffer (0.01 M PBS with 1 % BSA, 120 μL/well) at RT for 1 h. Following another rinsed with the blocking buffer, 120 µL of Ara h 3 standard solutions (0.1 M Tris–HCl buffer containing different concentration of Ara h 3, 0∼2000 ng·mL−1, pH 8.0) or sample extracts were added and incubated for 1 h. After washing steps, 120 µL of P43 with both tags in PBS (detection antibody, 10 μg·mL−1) were added to the coated plate and incubated for 1 h. After a further wash, 120 μL of mouse anti-HA IgG was added and incubated for 1 h at RT. After removing and washing steps, 120 μL of HRP-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgGs were added and incubated for another 60 min. The above operations were all carried out at RT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The method applied for real food specimens
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the accuracy and precision of the proposed method, the recovery rates of spiked specimens were calculated and recorded. The samples were all collected from the local supermarket in Tianjin, China. The pretreatment of above samples was elaborated in the Supporting Information. After pretreatment, the developed immunoassay was applied to the Ara h 3 determination in spiked food samples. Each analysis was performed in triplicate.

In this work, APBA was selected as the carbon source, nitrogen source and boron source for the preparation of B/N­CDs. In high pressure and high temperature, the structure of APBA was broken up to shape the carbon dots (Shen & Xia, 2014). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) were used to characterize the surface morphology and the distribution of B/N-CDs. In Fig. S1A, the observable regular black spots with clear lattices (lattice interval was 0.23 nm) indicated the formation of B/N-CDs, which were in accord with the graphite 100 (Shen & Xia, 2014). As shown in Fig. S1B, the B/N-CDs were well-distributed with the average size of 2.37 nm.

The FT-IR measurement was implemented to the characterization of the B/N-CDs surface structure, as displayed in Fig. S1C. The distinct peak at 3380 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrational peak for NH group, in response to the peaks at 3380 and 3470 cm−1 of APBA. Furthermore, two vibration absorption bands were observed at 1607 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 corresponding to the presence of CN functional groups. Five bands at 1374 cm−1, 1261 cm−1, 1155 cm−1, 1073 cm−1 and 995 cm−1 were relevant to the stretching vibration bands of B−O, CO−C, C−OH, C−B and B−OH, respectively (Shen & Xia, 2014; Tian, He, Ge, & Song, 2017). And the OH stretching vibration (650–930 cm−1) was presented in the spectrum. The above characteristic peaks suggested that the presence of –OH, COOH, −NH2, C═O, groups on the surface of B/N-CDs. The surface functional groups may help B/N-CDs hydrophilic and stable in aqueous solution (C.-L. Shen, Su, Zang, Li, Lou, & Shan, 2017). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to demonstrate the surface components of B/N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1D, the full-scan XPS spectrum shows four dominant peaks at 191.6 eV, 284.8 eV, 398.8 eV and 531.9 eV, which are distributed to B 1s, C 1 s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively (Lillo, Romero, Portolés, Diez, Caregnato, & Gonzalez, 2015; Xiao et al., 2018). Besides, the descriptive XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S3A) demonstrated the existence of CO (286.4 eV, 286.2 eV), CN (285.3 eV), and CC/C═C (284.6 eV, 284.8 eV), C-B (283.9 eV) groups, respectively (Ge et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016). The high-resolution survey scan spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S3B) showed three peaks, which were ascribed to pyridinic N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic N (399.1 eV), and graphitic N (400.8 eV) (Feng, Chen, Han, Liu, Ren, & Chen, 2016; Sadhanala & Nanda, 2015). Moreover, The B 1s spectrum (Fig. S3C) exhibited two peaks at 192.7 and 191.8 eV, which were attributed to B-O and B-C bonds.

The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of B/N-CDs were also recorded in Fig. 2 . The UV–Vis spectra exhibited two remarkable peaks at 235 and 290 nm, which were respectively owing to n∼π* and π∼π* transition of CC–N and CC (Fig. 2A). Also, the fluorescence spectrum of B/N-CDs displayed the dominant emission at 430 nm under different light source irradiation (320–390 nm) (Fig. 2B). Inset in Fig. 2B shows the B/N-CDs solution was yellow with irradiation by sunlight, which emitted intense blue fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation (365 nm). The characteristics of the emission/absorption spectra agreed well with those of B/N-CDs in the previous report, confirming the successful preparation of B/N-CDs (Xiao et al., 2018). The fluorescence stability of B/N-CDs was also studied after enduring various pH conditions or at different temperature in 1 h (Fig. S3A and S3B). B/N-CDs provided an exceptional optical stability, Fig. S3C displayed that the normalized fluorescence intensity of B/N-CDs were barely changed with sustaining irradiation for 60 min. As shown in Fig. S3D, the stability of B/N-CDs could be kept at 4 ℃ for above 30 days. B/N-CDs still maintained enough fluorescence intensity for detection as a whole. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of B/N-CDs remained static without significant variation, certificating that the fluorescence of B/N-CDs behaved steadily. Therefore, B/N-CDs were available for fabricating a novel immunoassay establishment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensing mechanism for Ara h 3
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensing method for Ara h 3 are illustrated in Fig. 1: HRP catalyzed the oxidizing reaction of colorless and non-fluorescent o-PD to DAP in the existence of H2O2, which showed the obvious fluorescence emission at 549 nm. When the reacted system of HRP/H2O2/o-PD was mixed with B/N-CDs, the emission peak of B/N-CDs at 426 nm had declined substantially, and strong emission at 549 nm was obtained. The data exhibited plainly that the HRP-mediated oxidic products (DAP) could lead to the fluorescence extinction of B/N-CDs. The fluorescence extinction of B/N-CDs was probably caused by IFE, which the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorophore interlapped the absorption spectrum of the quencher (Tian et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 3 A, DAP had an absorption peak at 431 nm, which has significant overlap with the emission band (426 nm) of B/N-CDs to generate IFE. Ulteriorly, to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and zeta potential were also determined. In Fig. 3B, under the addition of B/N-CDs, the UV–vis spectra of DAP have changed little, which indicated that fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between DAP and B/N-CDs was excluded (L. Wang et al., 2022). As displayed in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence lifetimes of B/N-CDs around the accession of DAP were 1.23 and 1.11 ns, individually. Nearly constant fluorescence lifetime illustrated no energy or electron transfer has taken place between B/N-CDs and DAP (Kumar et al., 2021; Q. Li, Guo, Gao, & Li, 2022). Besides, the zeta potentials of DAP and B/N-CDs distributed in NaAc buffer (0.01 M, pH 5.5) were −10.67 and −5.44 mV, severally. Owing to the maintained negative charges of B/N-CDs and DAP, the electrostatic adsorption did not occur between B/N-CDs and DAP. This indicated that the distance between them was hardly shorter than 10 nm. Accordingly, the FRET between DAP and B/N-CDs was extremely unlikely to happen (W. Wang, Ji, Kapur, Zhang, & Mattoussi, 2015). On the basis of these results, the ascertained fluorescence quenching was attributed to IFE between DAP and B/N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimal conditions for the detection
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimum performance toward Ara h 3, some important parameters were optimized, including the reactant concentrations, the pH value, and reaction time. There was almost none fluorescence quenching occurred in the emission of B/N-CDs when they existed separately with different concentrations of H2O2, o-PD or HRP (Fig. S4).

Furthermore, the impacts of o-PD concentrations in catalyzed reaction were investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity ratio of DAP to B/N-CDs (f549 /f426 ). With HRP as catalyst in hydrogen peroxide, the value of f549 /f426 increased rapidly and reached balance when the concentrations of o-PD were at 5 mM with increasing o-PD concentrations (Fig. S5A). Also, the balance reached when the concentration of H2O2 was at 5 mM (Fig. S5B). Therefore, 5 mM of o-PD and H2O2 were selected as the optimum concentration. The pH effect was investigated in the pH range from 4 to 10.0 in Fig. S5C, the fluorescence intensity ratio (f549 /f426 ) reached a maximum value in NaAC buffer (pH 5.5), thus pH 5.5 was selected as the optimal pH value for fluorescence method. The f549 /f426 changed slightly 10 min later when B/N-CDs were added (Fig. S5D), thus, 10 min was selected as the appropriate incubation time for Ara h 3 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Ara h 3
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the importance of monitoring peanut allergen concentrations in food, we applied this novel method to detect Ara h 3. As the increasing of Ara h 3 concentrations, the value of f549 /f426 went up gradually in Fig. 4 A. Further, Fig. 4B showed the relationship between f549 /f426 and the concentrations of Ara h 3. An appropriate linear correlation was obtained in the Ara h 3 concentration range of 10∼1200 ng mL−1, with a regression equation:
(2)
y
f 549 / f 426
= 0.0008
C
A r a h 3
+ 0.1748
where the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9908. Moreover, it suggested that the absorption intensities (U431 ) accumulated along with the increased concentrations of Ara h 3 (Fig. 4C and D). The linear response for Ara h 3 detection ranged from 30 to 1500 ng mL−1, with a regression equation:
(1)
U 431
= 0.0009
C
A r a h 3
+ 0.1736
where R2 was 0.9904 in Fig. 4C. The detection limits (LODs) for Ara h 3 using the colorimetry and radiometric fluorescence method based on 3σ/k was were calculated to be 6.61 and 9.79 ng mL−1, respectively. Where σ is the relative standard deviation (RSD) of ten replicate measurements of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity or ultraviolet absorption of the blank samples, and k is the slope of the calibration plots. The LODs for Ara h 3 using the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence methods were calculated, respectively. In contrast with some published methods for food allergens determination (Table 1 ), the proposed dual-mode immunoassay is comparable or even superior to other methods. The LOD of colormetric method was approximately 5.4-fold lower than the sandwich ELISA, which meets the requirements for testing the products of Ara h 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the dual-mode immunoassay for Ara h 3 determinaton

ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability of this method, we detected Ara h 3 in real food samples. The peanut allergy Ara h 3 and fried peanuts were both added directly into the real foods (peanut ingredients are not displayed on food packaging). The corresponding results were obtained as displayed in Table S2 and Table S3. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of Ara h 3 were ranging 82.4 % to 108.6 %, and the values of RSD were almost below 6 %, indicating presentable precision and accuracy in the processes. Table S3 also showed available recoveries which the peanuts are directly added to food. These results demonstrated that the proposed method was applicable for Ara h 3 monitoring in real samples.

Based on B/N-CDs, colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence immunoassay for peanut allergen Ara h 3 using o-PD as peroxidase substrate has been developed. This dual-mode immunoassay possesses several obvious advantages: (1) B/N-CDs were prepared without the complicated synthesis processes; (2) the immunoassay can output colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent dual-readout signal, which improving the accuracy of the proposed ELISA; (3) Based on the identification of nanobody, the proposed ELISA with highly specificity and sensitivity is simple and flexible. This work broke through the limitations including narrow detection limits, insufficient specificity and sensitivity, significant cross-reaction of conventional antibodies, and insufficient reproducibility. Furthermore, this paper covers a successful application of a two-dimensional quantification ELISA for the detection of Ara h 3 concentrations in food with satisfactory results. Therefore, we believe that the applicability of this method can be further expanded in food allergen detection.
This work was financially supported by the National Key R&D Program of China, grant number 2022YFF1102400.

Chixuan Yao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yaozhong Hu: Methodology, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Qisijing Liu: Methodology, Data curation. Jing-Min Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xuemeng Ji: Conceptualization, Methodology. Huan Lv: Methodology, Data curation. Shuo Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: 1. Instruments and Real samples pretreatment. 2. Figures.. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137679.
